story:
Robert Patton pays the cabbie and steps into a street gone strangely dark too early. The sky splits open. Black clouds tumble, and the rain comes down in a blinding sheet—wind howling, no lights, no people, no traffic, just the downpour. He has the driver drop him near the chaos of a spectacular uptown accident, hoping to quiet his mood, to get away from the whole day. Soaked and shivering, he spots a faint neon beer sign across the deserted street and slips into a tavern.

Inside is gloom and chill, the kind of cold that seeps through wet clothes and settles in the bones. The room is dim, worn down, and smells of old spills and older neglect. Everything’s on its last legs—except the bar itself, which gleams like an island of polish and brass. Glass pyramids sparkle; bottles line up in clean, lustrous ranks. The bartender, a slender man in a red vest and black bow tie, smiles pleasantly and says his name is Luke.

The patrons don’t move. No TV, no music, no talk—just figures stalled over their drinks. Patton feels as if he’s slipped into another world. He asks where this is. “This is Omega,” Luke says. “Up front they call it the cafe; in back, the bar.” Patton gives his name—Robert Patton—and with it comes a rising, uncoiling stream of his life: the deals, the people, the years; the loneliness creeping in; his small empire starting to crumble under treachery; the hard-luck stretch that’s haunted him lately.

Patton asks why everyone’s so quiet. Luke says they’ve lost their dreams. Everyone starts with dreams—people, places, happenings they want. These folks used theirs up. They chose their lives, spent their chances, and there’s nothing left to look for or talk about. Patton argues the way he knows how: there’s a winner and a loser in every deal, and the point is to win. Luke presses—what about all the men and women, the kids and the old, the rich and the poor he’s dealt with? Patton snaps: it doesn’t add up to anything. Yet a sharp, private memory of a public humiliation flashes through him, and he shudders.

Luke tells him the rest. “This is the Omega Bar, downtown—way downtown, by the river. The end. These people sit and wait for the payback of wasted lives, trapped in the shame they can’t forget, reliving it forever. I take them across.” He looks at Patton. “You were in that accident uptown. You’re out of time too.” Luke draws him a fifty-cent beer.

The door bangs open, wind and rain at its back. A man in a wet trench coat and fedora strides in, voice smooth—a street kid polished up. He calls Patton by name. “I’m Michael. You’re not going with him.” The Boss saw Patton drag a kid out of the way before the truck hit him, and decided there was some good in him. Michael says he’s taking Patton back to the hospital. Patton’s been hurt, but he’ll survive. Luke’s pleasant smile curdles. Michael chuckles. “Yeah, Luke. How about ‘Lucifer’.”

The gleaming bar, the motionless drinkers, the rain like a curtain outside—Patton realizes he’s stepped between worlds. One promised a cheap beer and forever. The other, a second chance.
